1: YHWH is flagged -- But now, this is what the LORD says -- he who created you, Jacob, and formed you, Israel: Do not be afraid, for I have redeemed you; I have called you by name; you are mine. The but now (ve-attah) after the judgment of Isaiah 42:24-25 is the hinge of grace. Three foundational acts: created, formed, redeemed. The personal claiming -- I have called you by name; you are mine -- is the covenant-ownership declaration. John 10:3 (the shepherd calls his own sheep by name) draws on this ownership-by-naming language.
2: When you pass through the waters, I will be with you -- the promise of divine presence through danger, not the promise of danger-free passage. The waters will not overwhelm, the rivers will not sweep away, the fire will not burn -- but the passing-through is real. The MKT's when (not if) is correct: these are the conditions of life in a fallen world, and the promise is company, not immunity. The water/fire pair creates a merism: through every kind of danger.
3: Elohim and YHWH are flagged -- I am the LORD your God, the Holy One of Israel, your Savior. I give Egypt as your ransom, Ethiopia and Seba in your place. The three divine titles (LORD your God, Holy One of Israel, your Savior) cluster around the ransom-declaration. The nations given as ransom for Israel is a stunning claim: the divine economy values Israel above the great nations. The ransom language is picked up by Jesus (Mark 10:45: the Son of Man came to give his life as a ransom for many).
4: Nefesh (soul, disp 3) is flagged -- I will give people in exchange for you and nations in exchange for your life (nafshekha). The nefesh here is Israel's life/being -- YHWH gives nations as the equivalent value. The logic: because you are precious and honored in my eyes and because I love you -- the triple ground of divine action is valuation, honor, and love. The MKT renders nafshekha as your life, correctly -- this is the vital-self, not merely the soul as inner spiritual component.
5: Do not be afraid, for I am with you. The same promise as 41:10 now frames the gathering from all four compass directions: east, west, north, south. The geographical comprehensiveness of the gathering mirrors the comprehensiveness of the diaspora -- wherever the exiles were scattered, the LORD will bring them back. The MKT's from the east...from the west and the corresponding north/south commands follow the Hebrew's deliberate exhaustiveness.
6: The command to the four directions to release and return the exiles: Give them back! Do not hold them back. The divine imperative directed at the compass points treats the whole created order as subject to YHWH's commands. My sons from far away, my daughters from the ends of the earth -- the parental address for the returning community. The MKT's from far away and from the ends of the earth create the widest possible scope.
7: Kavod (glory, disp 2) is flagged -- everyone who bears my name, whom I created for my glory, whom I formed and made. Three creation-verbs (created, formed, made) building on the foundational claim of v1. The creation for glory (likhvodi) makes human existence teleologically oriented toward divine glory -- as Paul develops in 1 Corinthians 6:20 (glorify God in your body). The MKT's created for my glory preserves this teleological dimension.
8: The paradoxical command: bring out the people who are blind -- though they have eyes -- and those who are deaf -- though they have ears. The theological blindness and deafness of Israel is the topic of the legal proceeding that follows: Israel is called as witness but is impaired as a witness because of its own spiritual incapacity. The though they have eyes / ears parentheses are the MKT's rendering of the Hebrew's concessive clause structure.
9: Emet (truth, disp 2) is flagged -- who among them declared this, or foretold us what is true? The nations are gathered for the lawsuit; they are challenged to produce witnesses who can declare the former things and announce what is true. Emet as the truth of predictive speech: a true prediction is one that comes to pass. The legal-epistemological framework treats truth as verifiable by historical fulfillment.
10: Aman (believe/trust, disp 2) and yada (know, disp 2) are flagged; YHWH is flagged -- You are my witnesses, declares the LORD, along with my servant whom I have chosen -- so that you may know and believe me and understand that I am he. The triple cognitive-response (know, believe/trust, understand) is the goal of the witness-vocation. Aman here means believe/trust -- the covenantal faith-response that follows knowing. The declaration I am he (ani-hu) is the divine identity formula of Second Isaiah, applied in John 8:24,28,58 to Christ.
11: YHWH is flagged -- I, I am the LORD, and apart from me there is no savior. The emphatic double first-person (I, I am) intensifies the divine self-identification. The exclusivity claim -- no savior apart from YHWH -- is the theological conclusion of the preceding witness-structure. Peter quotes this exclusivity language for Christ (Acts 4:12: no other name under heaven...by which we must be saved), applying the Isaianic uniqueness-of-YHWH formula to the risen Jesus.
12: YHWH is flagged -- I declared, I saved, and I proclaimed. The three divine past-actions that constitute the evidence for the claim of v11. Then: You are my witnesses, declares the LORD, and I am God. The witness-declaration closes the section: Israel has witnessed the declaring, saving, and proclaiming; therefore Israel is qualified to testify. The MKT's and I am God is a rendering of ve-ani El (and I am God/El) -- the generic term for deity rather than the personal name, emphasizing the theological claim.
13: Even from eternity I am he. The mi-yom (from the day / from ever) anticipates the eternal self-identification formula. No one can deliver from my hand. When I act, who can reverse it? The irreversibility of divine action is the guarantor of the salvation promises: what YHWH saves cannot be unsaved by any other power. The MKT's who can reverse it renders yeshivenah: who can turn it back?
14: YHWH is flagged -- your Redeemer, the Holy One of Israel. The go'el (kinsman-redeemer) title applied to YHWH: the one who has the family obligation to redeem is the LORD himself. The redemption is specifically from Babylon -- YHWH sends against Babylon and brings the Chaldeans down as fugitives. The Redeemer-from-Babylon is the new Exodus figure: as YHWH redeemed from Egypt, so from Babylon.
15: YHWH and melek (king, disp 2) are flagged -- I am the LORD, your Holy One, Israel's Creator, your King. The four divine titles in sequence: LORD (covenant name), Holy One (transcendence), Creator (cosmological sovereignty), King (political/covenantal authority). The MKT renders all four, with your King as the closing title -- making YHWH's kingship over Israel personal and direct. This is the answer to the foreign kings of Isaiah 36 who claimed sovereignty.
16: YHWH is flagged -- This is what the LORD says -- he who makes a way through the sea and a path through the mighty waters. The Exodus-defining act (splitting the Red Sea) is cited as the credential for what follows. The divine track record of sea-path making is the guarantee of the new path through the wilderness (v19). The MKT's mighty waters preserves the Hebrew mayim azim: strong/powerful waters.
17: The Exodus reversal: chariot and horse, army and warrior brought out -- then they lay down, never to rise. The Egyptian army's drowning (Exodus 14:27-28) is recalled as the paradigm case of YHWH's military action on Israel's behalf. The MKT's lay down and could not rise; they were extinguished, snuffed out like a wick uses the same wick-imagery as Isaiah 42:3 (the smoldering wick the Servant will not snuff out) -- but reversed for the enemies: they are snuffed out.
18: Do not remember the former things, or consider the events of long ago. The command to forget the Exodus is startling -- Isaiah is consistently Exodus-minded. But the point is: the new thing about to be done (v19) will so surpass the Exodus that clinging to the former thing as the supreme category of divine action would prevent recognizing the greater thing. The MKT's events of long ago renders qadmoniot: ancient/former things.
19: Yada (know, disp 2) is flagged -- Do you not see it? The new thing: the LORD is doing a new thing even now, springing up. The way through the wilderness and rivers in the desert are the material shape of the new Exodus. Do you not know/perceive it? -- the yada is the same perception-knowing as throughout the chapter. The new thing is already beginning and the question is whether anyone is perceiving it. The MKT's do you not see it renders the rhetorical invitation to perceive the divine action already in progress.
20: Wild animals will honor YHWH -- jackals and ostriches -- because the LORD provides water in the wilderness and rivers in the desert for the chosen people to drink. The wild animals' response is the creation-praise that C.S. Lewis develops in The Lion, the Witch and the Wardrobe as Narnia responding to Aslan. Even the inhospitable creatures of desolation acknowledge the water-giving LORD. The MKT preserves the specificity: jackals and ostriches, both desert-haunt animals (cf. Isaiah 34:13-15).
21: The people I formed for myself, that they might declare my praise. The teleological statement: Israel was formed for the purpose of divine praise-declaration. This is the positive counterpart to the failure of vv22-24: the purpose is praise, the reality is silence and unfaithfulness. The MKT's formed for myself renders yatzarti-li: formed for my own sake -- the creation is purposive, not incidental.
22: Yet, Jacob, you have not called on me; you have grown weary of me, O Israel. The contrast with v21 is sharp: formed to declare praise but not calling on the LORD. The weariness (yagata) is the opposite of the renewal-of-strength promised for those who wait on the LORD (40:31 uses the same root). The covenant people are weary of the covenant God -- the deep failure of the relationship.
23: You have not brought sheep for burnt offerings -- the ritual accusations: the sacrificial system has been neglected, but not because YHWH was demanding too much (v23b: I have not burdened you with grain offerings or wearied you with frankincense). The problem is not the burden of the requirements but the people's failure to fulfill them. The MKT's not honored me renders lo kabadtani: not glorified/honored me -- the kavod that belongs to the LORD has not been given.
24: You have not bought me fragrant calamus with money, nor satisfied me with the fat of your sacrifices. Instead you have burdened me with your sins and wearied me with your iniquities. The reversal: the people have not offered their required gifts, but they have given YHWH their sins as an unwanted gift. The MKT's burdened me and wearied me (heevadtani...helo'tani) use the same language as the acceptable worship vocabulary -- inverted. The worship-absence has been filled with sin-presence.
25: YHWH is flagged -- I, I am the one who blots out your transgressions for my own sake, and I will not remember your sins. The emphatic double first-person (anoki anoki hu) makes the forgiveness an act of divine self-motivation: the LORD blots out sins for my own sake (lema'ani), not because Israel deserves it or has offered adequate atonement. The formula I will not remember your sins is the basis of the new covenant in Jeremiah 31:34 and Hebrews 10:17. The MKT's rendering preserves the emphatic self-referentiality of the forgiveness.
26: Shaphat (contend, disp 2) is flagged -- Let us argue the matter together. Present your case, that you may be proved innocent. The courtroom resumes after the accusation of vv22-24 and the forgiveness of v25: can you prove your innocence? The MKT's argue the matter together renders nishpatah yachdav: let us contend/judge together. The invitation to argue your case is a form of grace -- the accused is given the opportunity to speak.
27: Your first ancestor sinned -- either Adam (the universal ancestor) or Abraham/Jacob (the specific covenant ancestor). The intermediaries (melakim: interpreters or go-betweens, possibly the prophets or priests who should have mediated covenant faithfulness) have rebelled. The long history of covenant failure extends from the first ancestor to every intermediary -- the people cannot claim their case has been defended.
28: Qodesh (holiness, disp 2) is flagged -- I allowed the sanctuary's leaders to be defiled, and I gave Jacob over to destruction and Israel to scorn. The holy princes (sarei qodesh) -- the priestly/levitical leaders -- defiled and the people given over to destruction. The qodesh applied to the leaders who were supposed to embody holiness but instead led to defilement. The MKT's sanctuary's leaders renders sarei qodesh: the holy/set-apart ones who lead. The chapter ends at the point of judgment -- the restoration promise of 44:1ff will reverse it.
